title: Lamb bhuna
subtitle: Thick, dry-fried lamb curry with caramelised spice paste
excerpt: Lamb pieces slow-fried in a thick, almost dry paste of deeply caramelised onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, and spices. A bhuna has very little sauce compared to other curries: the technique is to fry the ingredients together repeatedly until the paste coats the meat and becomes intensely flavoured. One of the most satisfying curry-house dishes.
sourceNotes: Bhuna (from Urdu bhunna, 'to fry') describes a dry-fry technique used across South Asian cooking where the masala is fried repeatedly until the oil separates and the paste is deeply caramelised. It appears throughout British curry-house menus as a preparation for lamb, chicken, and prawns.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Reheat with a splash of water.

content.text: A bhuna is defined by its technique: the masala paste is fried in oil until it darkens and the oil rises to the surface — a sign that the water in the vegetables has evaporated and the paste is properly cooked. This is repeated after adding each set of ingredients. The result is a sauce with intense, concentrated flavour and very little liquid. The lamb pieces become coated in the paste rather than surrounded by gravy.

The oil separation is a useful visual guide: when you can see the ghee or oil pooling at the surface and around the edges of the pan after frying the onion paste, the base is ready for the next stage. Don't rush past this point; it's where the depth of flavour comes from.

Heat the {{ghee}} in a wide, heavy pan over high heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 20 minutes, stirring frequently, until deep brown and beginning to crisp at the edges. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and fry for 2 minutes. Add the {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{chilli-powder}}, and {{turmeric}} and fry for 1 minute, stirring constantly. Fry until the oil separates out around the edges of the paste.

Add the {{lamb-shoulder}} pieces and fry over high heat for 5 minutes until browned on all sides. Add the {{tomato}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until they break down and the oil separates again. Season with salt. Add 200 ml water, cover, and cook over low heat for 50 to 60 minutes until the lamb is tender. Remove the lid for the final 10 minutes to reduce any excess liquid. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and top with fresh coriander if using.

items.cause: Lid on too long or too much water added
items.fix: A bhuna should be thick and dry. Remove the lid and boil off excess liquid. The final dish should coat the back of a spoon rather than pool on the plate

items.cause: Heat too high or not enough stirring
items.fix: After adding the tomatoes, stir frequently. If the paste starts to stick, add a splash of water and reduce the heat slightly

items.symptom: Lamb is still tough at 60 minutes
items.cause: Shoulder cut into large pieces or cooked at too low a simmer
items.fix: Keep the liquid at a steady simmer throughout. Add a splash more water if needed and continue for another 20 minutes
The 20-minute onion cook is the foundation. They should be a deep mahogany brown — not just golden. This is where the characteristic bhuna flavour comes from

content.text: Bhuna is a favourite of curry-house regulars who want depth over sauce: it is the choice of someone who knows what they want rather than what they order on a first visit. On the British curry-house menu it sits alongside the other dry preparations (dopiaza, pathia) as a contrast to the cream-based and sauce-based curries. The technique of oil separation as a quality check is shared across Indian subcontinental cooking and is the same principle behind a good rogan josh base or a Bangladeshi tarka.
